titleStmt.title: Letter from Emma Armstrong to Lady Clonbrock, 28 July 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Emma Armstrong

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Emma Armstrong to Augusta Caroline Dillon, Lady Clonbrock (1840-1928). Clonbrock was a prominent member of the Irish Women's Association, founded to provide aid and assistance to Irish soldiers and prisoners of war and the wife of Luke Gerald Dillon, 4th Baron Clonbrock. In the letter, Armstrong thanks Clonbrock for her letter. She goes on to discuss the case of Mr Donnell and matters relating to some soldiers. Armstrong then states that she is keeping well and wishes to see Lady Clonbrock when she returns also discussing the Soldiers and Sailors Help Society (SSHS). The letter goes on to discuss Armstrong's work, stating that it is too much for her, for which she suggests a man is appointed.

msIdentifier.collection: Clonbrock Estate Papers, Collection List No. 54; Ms. 35,796/10/10

correspAction.placeName: Mount Pleasant Avenue, Ballinasloe, County Mayo, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Mount Pleasant Avenue
Ballinasloe Jul 28 16 My dear Lady Clonbrock Thanks for your letter —
M Mc Donnell — Colonel Oats
is Taking up his case with
Thurs — meanwhile he is
getting 10/- from me, & 10/- a
week from his insurance
daily & so he is well off at the
present — he used to be a good
writer & also I regret to say
a good drinker he was at
Tipperary but did not harm
any Trade said he had not
not been as he was here
message — no Commandant
there until he did not care
to harm me as they have to
do believe their drills &
things — Murray says he cannot
wear his leg & that he told
so at Roehampton
I believe &
are both coming
well as soon as they
can scrape the money together
to get back — I fear all
the men are hopeless
We are both keeping well .
When you come back I
want to see Lord Clonbrock
& say see who he could
get to take up the County
writ for S S N S . I am
quite willing to keep on
Ballinasloe — but this place is getting too much for me
& I don't like the responsibility
of it — Would Sir Wn Mahon
do it do you think he would
be very good & I do think
it might take a man
in position — not a woman.
M<hi rend="superscript><hi rend="underline>c Donnell Ballinasloe With kind regards
Yours vy sincerely Emma Armstrong
